elements.sent-2411-001: Transformer model has gained prominence as a popular deep neural network architecture for neural language processing (NLP) and computer vision (CV) applications.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the extensive use of nonlinear operations, like softmax, poses a performance bottleneck during transformer inference and comprises up to 40\% of the total latency.
elements.sent-2411-003: Hence, we propose innovations at the circuit, architecture, and algorithm levels to accelerate the transformer.
elements.sent-2411-004: At the circuit level, we propose topkima—combining top-$k$ activation selection with in-memory ADC (IMA) to implement a low-energy and low-latency softmax without any sorting latency.
elements.sent-2411-005: Only the $k$ largest activations are sent to the softmax calculation block, reducing the huge computational cost of softmax.
elements.sent-2411-006: Using a modified training scheme with top-$k$ only in the forward pass, experimental results demonstrate only a 0.4\% to 1.2\% reduction in accuracy across ViT, distilBERT, and BERT-base models when evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SQuAD datasets with $k$=5.
elements.sent-2411-007: At the architecture level, an improved scale-free technique is introduced to reduce the computational cost of attention.
elements.sent-2411-008: The combined system, dubbed Topkima-Former, enhances $1.8\times-84\times$ speedup and $1.3\times-35\times$ energy efficiency (EE) over prior In-memory computing (IMC) accelerators.
elements.sent-2411-009: Compared to a conventional softmax macro and a digital top-$k$ (Dtopk) softmax macro, our proposed tokima softmax macro achieves about $15\times$ and $8\times$ faster speed respectively.
elements.sent-2411-010: Transformer can achieve superior performance in various applications involving neural language processing (NLP) and computer vision (CV) due to the attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, as the models evolve, the required computational and memory resources increase rapidly.
elements.sent-2411-012: To efficiently implement transformer on hardware, many software (SW) and hardware (HW) techniques have been proposed to accelerate the linear dot-product operation.
elements.sent-2411-013: On the SW side, there are two categories: (1) Quantization, where the weights and activations of matrix multiplications are quantized to low precision data \cite{yang2020retransformer .}
elements.sent-2411-014: On the HW front, In-memory computing (IMC) is a popular method that reduces data movement to and from memory.
elements.sent-2411-015: Both volatile (SRAM, DRAM) and nonvolatile (RRAM, Flash) solutions have been explored.
elements.sent-2411-016: Resistive memory approaches have gained more traction recently due to their possibility of low-energy combined with low-area implementation above the active silicon area.
elements.sent-2411-017: These methods can implement vector-matrix multiplies very efficiently \cite{sridharan2023x , but they suffer from ADC overheads \cite{peng2020dnn+ .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: To address this challenge, In-memory ADC (IMA) is proposed to embed ADC into each row of the IMC macro \cite{yu202016k .}
elements.sent-2411-019: This approach allows for the reuse of bit cells for both multiply-accumulate (MAC) and ADC operations, resulting in enhanced energy efficiency (EE).
elements.sent-2411-020: However, the latency is slightly high due to the utilization of ramp ADC, which requires $2^n$ cycles for an $n$-bit ADC.
elements.sent-2411-021: On the contrary, few works focus on optimizing the nonlinear (NL) operations in transformer, like softmax, which can result in a large overhead in latency and energy.
elements.sent-2411-022: For example, the softmax operation consumes up to 40\% inference time, severely impeding the efficient implementation of transformer \cite{stevens2021softermax .}
elements.sent-2411-023: To solve this problem, low-precision implementation changes the base of exponential from $e$ to 2, achieving $1.25\times$ speed improvement \cite{zhang2022base .}
elements.sent-2411-024: And using Taylor series to approximate exponentials reduces inference time by only 19\% \cite{nilsson2014hardware .}
elements.sent-2411-025: The lack of significant improvement can be attributed to the necessity of handling all inputs of the softmax function.
elements.sent-2411-026: This becomes particularly challenging when dealing with lengthy inputs.
elements.sent-2411-027: Motivated by competitive networks in neuroscience \cite{wta , this work integrates the concept of top-$k$ winner take all with IMA (topkima) such that only $k$ ($\ll d$) values need to be passed to the NL operator.}
elements.sent-2411-028: This works well since the output of the softmax exponentially increases larger values over others.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our proposed method eliminates sorting overheads and even reduces the latency overhead of ramp ADC.
elements.sent-2411-030: This design fully harnesses the potential of top-$k$, significantly reducing the overhead of the softmax operation in transformers.
elements.sent-2411-031: To further accelerate transformer, the integrated design, dubbed Topkima-Former, spans circuit, architecture, and algorithm innovations, which has the following main features:.
elements.sent-2411-032: Circuit: Integrating sorting operation for top-$k$ selection in the IMA array without sorting overhead.
elements.sent-2411-033: This is achieved by replacing the increasing ramp in prior works with a decreasing ramp, allowing it to cross larger MAC voltages earlier.
elements.sent-2411-034: The integrated topkima can save energy and latency in both the data conversion stage (by early stopping) and the subsequent softmax operation by processing a reduced number of values.
elements.sent-2411-035: Algorithm: Modified training scheme where $k$=5 results in only a 0.4\% to 1.2\% reduction in accuracy across ViT, distilBERT, and BERT-base models when evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SQuAD datasets.
elements.sent-2411-036: Architecture: Removing scaling operations by just adjusting the weights of $W_Q$ without any overhead.
elements.sent-2411-037: Topkima-Former achieves $1.8\times-84\times$ faster and $1.3\times-35\times$ more EE than prior IMC-based accelerators.
elements.sent-2411-038: Compared to a conventional softmax macro and a digital top-$k$ (Dtopk) softmax macro, our proposed topkima softmax macro (topkima-SM) achieves about $15\times$ and $8\times$ faster speed.
elements.sent-2411-039: Transformer models are built up with multiple encoders and/or decoders.
elements.sent-2411-040: Multi-head attention is the main component of each coder.
elements.sent-2411-041: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig1 , there are three weight matrixes in one attention module: $W_Q$, $W_K$ and $W_V$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Then Queries ($Q$), Keys ($K$), and Values ($V$) could be obtained by multiplying the input $X$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:transformer} Q, K, V=X\cdot W_Q, X\cdot W_K,X\cdot W_V\end{equation} where $W_Q, W_K \in \mathbb{R ^{d_{model}\times d_k}$, $W_V \in \mathbb{R ^{d_{model}\times d_v}$, $X\in \mathbb{R ^{{SL}\times d_{model }$, $Q, K\in \mathbb{R ^{SL\times d_k}$.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:transformer} Q, K, V=X\cdot W_Q, X\cdot W_K,X\cdot W_V\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-043: The result of this attention module is calculated by: \begin{equation}\label{eq:attention} Attention(Q,K,V)=softmax(\frac{Q \cdot K^T}{\sqrt{d_k}})\cdot V\end{equation} where the dimension of the result $\in \mathbb{R ^{d_k\times d_v}$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: These NL operations render softmax difficult for HW implementation \cite{dass2023vitality .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Softmax involves expensive operations of exponential and division.
elements.sent-2411-049: The popular solution is to use function approximation to reduce the overhead of these two operations.
elements.sent-2411-050: Softermax switches the base of exponential from $e$ to 2 to simplify HW implementations \cite{stevens2021softermax .}
elements.sent-2411-051: I-BERT introduces a more general approximation, replacing the exponential with 2nd-order polynomials \cite{kim2021bert .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Similarly, SpAtten employs a 5th-order Taylor approximation \cite{wang2021spatten .}
elements.sent-2411-053: In addition to the approximation of the exponential, Du et al. utilize the log sum-exp trick to avoid the division operation \cite{du2019efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-054: However, the aforementioned schemes require processing all inputs for softmax, leading to considerable latency and energy consumption when the input size ($SL$) is very large.
elements.sent-2411-055: For example, latency increases drastically by $137\times$ when $SL$ increases from $256$ to $4096$ \cite{du2019efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Top-$k$ method has also been proposed to process only the $k$ largest inputs of softmax \cite{peng2022length .}
elements.sent-2411-057: It can achieve $2.6\times$ speedup when $k$=30 with a small loss in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-058: However, it suffers from the sorting complexity of $O(SL)$.
elements.sent-2411-059: In our estimation, the sorting operation consumes at least $75\%$ latency (See Section \ref{sec:HW_evaluation ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: Retransformer leverages the promising RRAM device to expedite the inference of transformer \cite{yang2020retransformer .}
elements.sent-2411-062: However, the low endurance of RRAM renders it unsuitable for deployment within crossbar arrays that require frequent programming, such as the crossbar $K^{T}$ with input $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-063: Conversely, TranCIM adopts the mature fabrication technology of SRAM to design a full-SRAM IMC accelerator for transformer \cite{tu2022trancim .}
elements.sent-2411-064: But this design forfeits benefits associated with RRAM, including high storage density, fast read speed and low energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-065: Subsequently, X-former proposes a hybrid IMC architecture built up with RRAM and SRAM together to efficiently execute different workloads of transformer \cite{sridharan2023x .}
elements.sent-2411-066: However, it lacks a comprehensive co-design from circuit level, to architecture level and up to algorithm level.
elements.sent-2411-067: We optimized Topkima-Former by using SW-HW co-design as described in this section.
elements.sent-2411-069: The overall system simulation, conducted using the NeuroSim framework \cite{peng2020dnn+ , comprises chip, tile, processing element (PE) and array hierarchies.}
elements.sent-2411-070: The weights ($W_{Q,K,V}$) of projection operations in Fig.~\ref{fig1 are mapped onto RRAM IMC because RRAM provides high storage density, fast read speed, and low energy consumption when performing vector-matrix multiplication with fixed weights.}
elements.sent-2411-071: However, SRAM is chosen for attention operations ($Q\cdot K^{T}$ and $A\cdot V$) since it needs to be written for every input sample.
elements.sent-2411-072: Therefore, $K^{T}$ and $V$ are written in the SRAM IMC for every input.
elements.sent-2411-073: Note that the design of the RRAM IMC and SRAM IMC for $A\cdot V$ are consistent with NeuroSim design.
elements.sent-2411-074: Our focus in this work is the second SRAM IMC-- topkima-SM that combines topkima macro (topkima-M) that uses SRAM array for $Q\cdot K^{T}$ together with a digital softmax, as explained next.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figs/Slide2.pdf} \caption{Topkima-M hardware: (a) Block diagram. (b) Concept of early stopping in topkima. (c) Circuit diagram detail of one column. (d) Basic multiplication of proposed dual 10T SRAM cell. (e) Example timing diagram for 3 columns with top-1 selected.} \label{fig2}
elements.sent-2411-075: As seen in the block diagram of topkima-M (Fig.~\ref{fig2 (a)), $K^T$ is stored in the dual 10T SRAM array while $Q\cdot K^{T}$ is performed by SRAM IMC where $Q$ values are sent as inputs by pulse-width modulating the WL, a commonly used technique \cite{sram-survey .}}
elements.sent-2411-076: To facilitate read-disturb free computing and signed inputs, four extra transistors (illustrated as the blue component in Fig.~\ref{fig2 (d)) are added to the basic 6T cell.}
elements.sent-2411-077: For ternary weight (-1($Q_{L}$=L, $Q_{R}$=H)/ 0(($Q_{L}$=L, $Q_{R}$=L))/ +1(($Q_{L}$=H, $Q_{R}$=L))) storage, dual(Left-Right) cells are used (Fig.~\ref{fig2 (d)).}
elements.sent-2411-078: The pre-charged bitlines (BL) are discharged in accordance with the MAC operation of $Q\cdot K^{T}$ as follows: when RWL+/RWL- is at high voltage, four blue transistors in either the L or R side will conduct based on high/low logic levels of signals $Q_{L}$, $\bar{Q_{L }$, $Q_{R}$ and $\bar{Q_{R }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: This will cause a discharge current to flow through the read BL ($RBL_L$ or $RBL_R$), resulting in a small voltage drop on these read BL, as the basic multiplication in the table of Fig.~\ref{fig2 (d).}
elements.sent-2411-080: The accumulation happens by addition of the voltage drops for all activated bitcells.
elements.sent-2411-081: In contrast, when the RWL+/RWL- is at low voltage, there are no voltage changes.
elements.sent-2411-082: Fig.~\ref{fig2 (c) shows a case where $K^T$ values are quantized into 4 bits where 1 bit is the sign value.}
elements.sent-2411-083: To achieve this by using ternary weight cell as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig2 (d), three cells are used to represent one weight, with the three corresponding input PWM signals scaled by the factors of 1, 2 and 4 to achieve binary scaling.}
elements.sent-2411-084: This allows for 15 levels of weight (-7 to 7), which is approximately equivalent to 4 bits precision.
elements.sent-2411-085: These MAC voltages ($V_1$ to $V_n$ in Fig.~\ref{fig2 (a)) are then quantized using a ramp IMA (which enables replica bitcells one by one to create the ramp voltage) \cite{yu202016k and a sense amplifier (SA) as the comparator.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: After the MAC operation is completed, $32$ pulses are simultaneously sent in one clock cycle (Fig.~\ref{fig2 (c)) to dual 10T SRAM, leading to a discharge of $RBL_R$ for setting initial ramp voltage and calibration as necessary (see \cite{yu202016k ).}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Subsequently, another $32$ ramp pulses (5 bits ADC) are sequentially sent over $32$ cycles to the dual 10T cells for ADC, causing a progressive discharge on $RBL_L$ with each clock cycle, thereby creating the ramp.
elements.sent-2411-088: The main novelty of the proposed design is that it can integrate the sorting operation for top-$k$ selection in this IMA array.
elements.sent-2411-089: This is done by first changing the increasing ramp in prior work \cite{yu202016k to a decreasing one.}
elements.sent-2411-090: The decreasing ramp crosses larger voltages earlier ($t_1<t_k$ if $V_1>V_k$ in Fig.~\ref{fig2 (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-091: This can be verified by the example of 3 columns array with top-1 selected in Fig.~\ref{fig2 (e), where the largest MAC result from column 3 leads to early stopping.}
elements.sent-2411-092: To determine which columns' SA got triggered in a clock cycle, an arbiter-encoder combination is used similar to address event representation (AER) \cite{aer-davis where the latched SA outputs are treated as requests (REQ) and the acknowledgment (ACK) signals are used to disable the SA.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Along with the encoded `address' of the column, the conversion cycle at which the ramp crossing occurred is stored as the ADC output in registers.
elements.sent-2411-094: Further, a counter tracks the number of requests and stops the data conversion early (before $2^n$ clock periods) when the count equals or exceeds ‘$k$’.
elements.sent-2411-095: In the rare case of count exceeding $k$ due to ties (many similar large values), the number of outputs is reduced to $k$ by giving preference to smaller column addresses.
elements.sent-2411-096: After obtaining the top-$k$ quantized values, they are sent to a digital softmax core \cite{geng2019hardware to get the scores $A$.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Note that we include the operations of $Q \cdot K^T$ and the following softmax in the complexity comparisons of topkima-SM done later.. Topkima-SM benefits.
elements.sent-2411-098: The benefit of our proposed method can be understood by considering the latency of a conventional softmax macro, $T_{conv-SM}$, given by: $T_{conv-SM =T_{wr +d\cdot (T_{pwm,inp +T_{ima +d\cdot T_{NL,dig )$ where $T_{wr}$ is the time to write the $K^T$ values in SRAM, $T_{pwm,inp}$ is the time to create WL pulses of $Q$ (generally $\propto 2^{n_b}$ where $n_b$ is the bit-width of input), $T_{ima}$ is the time for data conversion by IMA and $T_{NL,dig}$ is the time taken for digital implementation of exponentiation and division.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Here, $T_{wr}$ occurs once while $d$ colmuns of $Q$ are applied one by one.
elements.sent-2411-100: For a top-$k$ approach, the latencies for digital sorting $T_{Dtopk-SM}$ and our proposed approach $T_{topkima-SM}$ are given by: \begin{equation}\label{eq:latency_topkima1} 0.9\hsize{!}{$ T_{Dtopk-SM}=T_{wr}+d\cdot(T_{pwm,inp}+T_{ima}+T_{sort}+k\cdot T_{NL,dig})$}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:latency_topkima2} 0.9\hsize{!}{$T_{topkima-SM}=T_{wr}+d\cdot(T_{pwm,inp}+T_{ima,arb}+k\cdot T_{NL,dig})$}\end{equation} where $T_{ima,arb =max(\alpha T_{ima +T_{arb ,T_{clk +k\cdot T_{arb )$ denotes latency of the IMA and arbiter-encoder in our proposed method.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: For both approaches, the NL compute time reduces by $d/k$ but an added time for sorting ($T_{sort =min(d\cdot log(d),d\cdot k)\times T_{clk}$) is required in the digital sorting case.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Additionally, the IMA latency in topkima reduces by a factor $\alpha$ due to early stopping of the ramp.
elements.sent-2411-103: Note that the term $T_{ima,arb}$ in \eqref{eq:latency_topkima2 includes one cycle of arbiter-encoder latency for the $k$-th or last ramp crossing in addition to $\alpha T_{ima}$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Practical crossbar dimensions can be smaller than the dimensions of $K^T$.
elements.sent-2411-106: In that case, the matrix $K^T$ has to be split and mapped into multiple crossbars.
elements.sent-2411-107: Two issues have to be handled in this case--(1) Reduced bit precision of $K^T$ since we have less number of SRAM rows in one column and (2) Inaccurate evaluation of top-$k$ since the top-$k$ selection for each array is now separate and there is no global information available.
elements.sent-2411-108: In this case, each array $i$ chooses top-$k_i$ such that $\sum k_i=k$.
elements.sent-2411-109: We refer to this technique as sub top-$k$ and show the resulting drop in accuracy in Section \ref{sec:results .}
elements.sent-2411-110: To mitigate the accuracy penalties of retaining only $k$ out of $d$ values in the top-$k$ method \cite{peng2022length , we especially propose a modified training method: top-$k$ forward-complete backward propagation (TFCBP), inspired by the quantization aware training \cite{yang2020retransformer .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Only top-$k$ activations are used to calculate softmax probabilities in the forward propagation stage, while all (i.e., $d$) activations participate in the gradient computation in the backward propagation stage.
elements.sent-2411-112: Moreover, since the latency and energy consumption of ramp IMA increases exponentially with the resolution, quantization-aware training (QAT) is introduced to reduce the activation precision \cite{yang2020retransformer .}
elements.sent-2411-113: To reduce accuracy drops, quantized activations are passed for forward propagation while the backward propagation exploits FP-32 based activations to update the weight.
elements.sent-2411-114: Note that these two methods are exclusively employed for training transformer in SW, following which the trained quantized network is mapped onto HW for inference.
elements.sent-2411-115: Scaling operation is needed to realize the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ in \eqref{eq:attention .}
elements.sent-2411-116: Each dot product result of $Q\cdot K^{T}$ requires a division operation, which introduces high design complexity in HW, such as combining bit shifting with constant multiplication\cite{yang2020retransformer .}
elements.sent-2411-117: Hence, we propose an improved scale-free design, inspired by \cite{afifi2023tron .}
elements.sent-2411-118: We rewrite the operation as: $\frac{Q\cdot K^T {\sqrt{d_k =\frac{X\cdot W_Q \cdot K^T {\sqrt{d_k =Q^s\cdot K^T$ where $Q^s =X\cdot \frac{W_Q {\sqrt{d_k }$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: So we can simply adjust the weights from $W_Q$ to $\frac{W_Q {\sqrt{d_k }$to achieve the scale function without any HW overhead for it{\cite{ran2020memristor .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: We first present SW design space exploration to show acceptable ranges of $k$ over several datasets.
elements.sent-2411-121: Next, HW performances from macro level, architecture level to system level are shown to present the benefits of using topkima in transformer.
elements.sent-2411-122: Lastly, the system performance is compared with state-of-the-art.
elements.sent-2411-123: At the SW level, we evaluate top-$k$ design with two types of transformers.
elements.sent-2411-124: For ViT, we run two representative datasets: CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, while for DistilBERT and BERT-base, SQuAD v1.1 (SQuAD) is run for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-125: Fig.~\ref{fig3 shows the result for varying $k$ from 1 to 20.}
elements.sent-2411-126: As expected, smaller $k$ indicates a more aggressive approximation, leading to a higher accuracy drop.
elements.sent-2411-127: For ViT on CIFAR-100 and DistilBERT / BERT-base on SQuAD, top-1 results in nonnegligible performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-128: However, top-5 can achieve a good trade-off between radical approximation and accuracy, with less than a 1.2\% accuracy drop.
elements.sent-2411-129: For ViT on CIFAR-10, top-1 can achieve comparable accuracy with the baseline (w/o top-$k$ approximation), with only 0.4\% accuracy loss.
elements.sent-2411-130: This significant advantage of our design over \cite{peng2022length in terms of radical approximation comes from the TFCBP training method.}
elements.sent-2411-131: The HW evaluation is done for BERT-base model on the SQUAD dataset, with 0.2\% accuracy loss after quantization.
elements.sent-2411-132: In this case, $Q\cdot K^T$ is evaluated using SPICE simulations in $65$ nm CMOS technology, where $Q$ and $K^T$ are quantized to $5$ bits and $4$ bits (15 levels of weight as detailed in Sec.\ref{sec:HW_opt ) respectively, using QAT.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Additionally, $X\cdot W_{Q,K,V}$ and $A\cdot V$ are evaluated using NeuroSim\cite{peng2020dnn+ .}
elements.sent-2411-134: In this evaluation, $W_{Q,K,V}$ are quantized into $8$ bits by post-training quantization, and both inputs $X$ and $A$, along with weight $V$, are quantized into 5 bits using QAT.

(b) Theoretical and simulated MAC value. (c) Impact of sub-top-$k$. (d) Different scale implementations. (e) Latency and (f) energy breakdown by components. (g) Latency and (h) energy breakdown by operations.} \label{fig4}
elements.sent-2411-135: Macro level analysis: Topkima-M SPICE simulation.
elements.sent-2411-136: First, we evaluate Topkima-M (computing $Q\cdot K^T$), followed by Topkima-SM which includes the subsequent softmax.
elements.sent-2411-137: The topkima-M with $n_b=5$ resolution for ADC selects the top-5 values from $384$ MAC results ($Q$ size of one head: $384\times 64$, $K^T$ size of one head: $64\times 384$).
elements.sent-2411-138: Due to the limitation of crossbar size, the weights $K^T$ and top-5 are split into $2$ parts: a ($64\times 3)\times256$ sub-crossbar with sub-top-3 ($k_1=3$) and a ($64\times 3)\times128$ sub-crossbar with sub-top-2 ($k_2=2$), where 3 pairs of bitcells represent 4 bits weight precision of $K^T$.
elements.sent-2411-139: Combined with $64$ replica bit cells per column (split evenly for calibration and ramp generation), the simulated sub-array size is set to $256\times 256$.
elements.sent-2411-140: Simulation of the arbiter, encoder, and counter across corners and power supply results in worst-case delays at SS corner and $V_{dd =0.8$ V of $1.51$, $0.57$, and $0.51$ ns respectively resulting in $T_{arb <2.08$ ns.}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Estimated from \cite{yang2020retransformer and \cite{du2019efficient , $T_{write =320$ ns (row-by-row parallel writing) and $T_{NL,dig =6.5$ ns.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Using a $2$ GHz clock for input 5 bits PWM results in maximum value of $T_{pwm,inp =15.5$ ns for the LSB and $T_{pwm,inp =62$ ns for the MSB.}}
elements.sent-2411-143: In Fig.~\ref{fig4 (a), the latency of topkima-SM, $T_{topkima-SM}$, is found to be $\approx 15\times$ and $8\times$ lesser than $T_{conv-SM}$ and $T_{Dtopk-SM}$ respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-144: The latency benefit of our design over conventional/Dtopk softmax mainly comes from a reduced number of inputs for the softmax operation and the elimination of the need to sort the top-$k$ values.
elements.sent-2411-145: Note that Dtopk does not improve much over conventional softmax due to the dominant sorting time overhead.
elements.sent-2411-146: Similarly, the energy consumption of our design $E_{topkima-SM}$ is $30\times$ and $3\times$ lesser than $E_{conv-SM}$ and $E_{Dtopk-SM}$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-147: The primary reason the EE improvement of our design over Dtopk is not as significant as the latency reduction is that the sorting energy is not a major contributor.
elements.sent-2411-148: Note that these speed/EE improvements increase with increasing $SL$ which bodes well for the scalability of this method (e.g. GPT 3.5 has $SL=4096$).

elements.sent-2411-149: Fig.~\ref{fig4 (b) shows the distribution of the IMA circuit output compared to SW calculations averaged across $256$ conversions.}
elements.sent-2411-150: The corresponding error was used to inject errors in SW simulations ($Q\cdot K^T$ and $A\cdot V$ which are mapped to SRAM) resulting in a small drop in accuracy from $86.7\%$ to $85.1\%$.
elements.sent-2411-151: We also assess the impact of crossbar size limitation on accuracy, by simulating with $128\times 128$ and $256\times 256$ crossbars.
elements.sent-2411-152: Specifically in Fig.~\ref{fig4 (c), when using $128\times 128$ crossbar to implement one head of $K^T$ (size: $64\times384$), 3 crossbars are required.}
elements.sent-2411-153: Each crossbar allocates 64 rows for ADC and then leaves only 64 rows for MAC operations, resulting in only ternary precision of $K^T$.
elements.sent-2411-154: Also, global top-5 ($k=5$) is used for four cases (Fig.~\ref{fig4 (e)) which gets divided into three sub-top-$k$: $k_1=2$, $k_2=2$ and $k_3=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-155: For example, if the output $Q\cdot K^T$ is [1, 2, 3, ..., 384], the selected values by three sub-top-$k$ are [127, 128], [255, 256] and [384] and then combined to be sent to digital softmax core for probability calculation.
elements.sent-2411-156: This is quite different from the selected values by global top-$k$: [380, 381, 382, 383, 384], leading to an accuracy drop.
elements.sent-2411-157: The case for $256\times 256$ crossbars are same as described earlier with two sub-top-$k$ arrays ($k_1=3$ and $k_2=2$) constituting the global top-5.
elements.sent-2411-158: Each crossbar has 64 rows allocated for ADC and calibration.
elements.sent-2411-159: This leaves 192 additional rows for MAC operations, increasing the precision of $K^T$ to 4 bits (See details in Section \ref{sec:HW_opt ).}
elements.sent-2411-160: It is observed that smaller crossbar size leads to larger accuracy degradation in Fig.~\ref{fig4 (c).}
elements.sent-2411-161: One reason is that a smaller crossbar size leads to reduced weight precision.
elements.sent-2411-162: Additionally, it fragments the global top-$k$ into more sub-top-$k$, thereby weakening the winner-takes-all principle.
elements.sent-2411-163: But for the datasets and networks considered here, commonly used crossbar size of $256\times256$ is sufficient to achieve an accuracy comparable to that of global top-$k$.. Architecture level analysis.
elements.sent-2411-164: To account for architecture/system-level overheads, a full attention module is simulated in NeuroSim following the architecture described in Section \ref{sec:HW_opt .}
elements.sent-2411-165: RRAM and SRAM technologies are adopted from \cite{fackenthal201419 and \cite{peng2020dnn+ respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-166: The SRAM array is programmed row-by-row, where the latency is one clock cycle per row and the dynamic power per cell is ${1.8\times10^{-7 }$ mW/MHz \cite{ramesh201190 .}}
elements.sent-2411-167: The peripheral configuration setting is the same as \cite{peng2020dnn+ .}
elements.sent-2411-168: The read pulse is 0.5 V, adopted from \cite{sridharan2023x .}
elements.sent-2411-169: Note that the peripheral overhead of topkima-M, like interconnect and buffer cost, are not provided in SPICE simulation.
elements.sent-2411-170: So these overheads are also estimated from Neurosim.
elements.sent-2411-171: Also, we only estimate one attention module from BERT-base on SQuAD to report the HW performance because transformer is built by stacking attention modules.
elements.sent-2411-172: As seen in Fig.~\ref{fig4 (d), we compare our scale-free design with left shift scale \cite{yang2020retransformer and Tron's free scale \cite{afifi2023tron , achieving $2.4\times$ and $1.5\times$ speedup respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-173: Left shift scale is not efficient due to the necessity of scaling for all elements from $Q\cdot K^{T}$ while Tron's free scale lacks parallelism and requires additional transpose operations.. System level analysis.
elements.sent-2411-174: To clearly demonstrate the benefits of topkima-SM on the entire system, we breakdown the latency and energy by components, as shown in Figs.~\ref{fig4 (e) and (f).}
elements.sent-2411-175: The latency/energy of softmax is significantly reduced after introducing topkima-SM.
elements.sent-2411-176: The synaptic array dominates the latency for two main reasons.
elements.sent-2411-177: First, the $4\times$ pulse width required for higher weight precision results in longer MAC times.
elements.sent-2411-178: Second, the MUX design in NeuroSim increases latency by determining which column's MAC output needs to be sent to the ADC \cite{peng2020dnn+ .}
elements.sent-2411-179: The buffer dominates the energy primarily because the 12 heads in the attention module require more buffers to store intermediate data.
elements.sent-2411-180: Unlike latency, the parallel operation of 12 heads does not conceal the energy overhead.
elements.sent-2411-181: Similarly, the latency and energy are broken down by operations in Figs.~\ref{fig4 (g) and (h).}
elements.sent-2411-182: We find that the speed of $X\cdot W_{Q,K,V}$ is about $2\times$ lower than $Q\cdot K^T$ and $A\cdot V$ due to the larger size of $W_{Q,K,V}$ compared to one attention head of $K^T$ and $V$.
elements.sent-2411-183: Although there are 12 heads, they can operate in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-184: However, the energy consumption of $Q\cdot K^T$ and $A\cdot V$ is dominant due to the 12 heads.
elements.sent-2411-185: The sparse input $A$ after topkima-softmax makes $A\cdot V$ more energy-efficient.
elements.sent-2411-186: Table 1 presents a comparison with state of the art accelerators, including traditional systems and IMC-based accelerators.
elements.sent-2411-187: Our proposed Topkima-Former can achieve $6.70$ TOPS throughput and $16.84$ TOPS/W EE at 200 MHz.
elements.sent-2411-188: Compared to ELSA \cite{ham2021elsa , ReTransformer \cite{yang2020retransformer , X-Former \cite{sridharan2023x and HARDSEA \cite{liu2023hardsea , Topkima-Former can achieve $1.8\times-84\times$ higher speed, and $1.3\times-35\times$ energy reduction, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: Note that no dedicated pipelining is introduced into Topkima-Former.
elements.sent-2411-190: Otherwise, the speed can be even faster.
elements.sent-2411-191: In this work, we propose Topkima-Former, co-designed from circuit level, architecture level to algorithm level.
elements.sent-2411-192: At circuit level, we propose topkima which can use IMA to select the top $k$ activation without any sorting latency and then achieve a low-energy and low-latency softmax.
elements.sent-2411-193: At algorithm level, $k$=5 can achieve satisfied accuracy with only a 0.4\%-1.2\% accuracy drop across ViT, distilBERT, and BERT-base models when evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SQuAD datasets due to the proposed TFCBP training method.
elements.sent-2411-194: At architecture level, we introduce a scale-free technique for removing expensive scale operations.
elements.sent-2411-195: The combined system can achieve $1.8\times-84\times$ faster and $1.3\times-35\times$ EE compared to prior IMC-based accelerators.
elements.sent-2411-196: In addition, the designed tokima-SM, demonstrates a $15\times$ and $8\times$ speedup compared to a conventional softmax macro and a Dtopk softmax macro, respectively.
